content:
Matplotlib grew from **MATLAB-inspired** plotting in Python. John Hunter began the project in **2002** to give scientific Python a native 2D graphics library; the first public releases established the **pyplot** interface familiar to MATLAB users.

The **object-oriented API** (Figure, Axes, Artist hierarchy) matured through the 2000s as NumPy became the array lingua franca. **IPython** and later **Jupyter** adoption made `%matplotlib inline` the default notebook experience, cementing Matplotlib in ML coursework and research workflows.

Major releases refined defaults: **Matplotlib 2.0** (2017) updated color cycles and styles; **3.0** (2018) tightened Python 3 support. The ecosystem layered **Seaborn** (2012 onward) and **pandas** `.plot()` on top rather than replacing the core.

For ML practitioners, Matplotlib became the **lingua franca for loss curves, histogram EDA, and scatter diagnostics**—while specialized topics (embedding visualization, confusion matrices, chart-type selection) split interpretation and statistical semantics into focused learning units.

Today Matplotlib remains the **rendering foundation** beneath most static Python ML figures, with optional style sheets (`plt.style.use('seaborn-v0_8-whitegrid')`) bridging aesthetics and the underlying Axes API taught here.

timeline.label: 2002 — project start
timeline.description: John Hunter launches Matplotlib to provide Python-native 2D plotting with a MATLAB-like pyplot interface.

timeline.label: 2000s — OO API and NumPy integration
timeline.description: Figure/Axes/Artist model solidifies; NumPy arrays become the standard input type for plot commands.

timeline.label: 2012 — Seaborn emerges
timeline.description: Statistical visualization layer builds on Matplotlib, popularizing higher-level EDA plots while leaving customization to Axes.

timeline.label: 2017–2018 — Matplotlib 2.0 / 3.0
timeline.description: Default style modernization and Python 3 focus; Jupyter inline rendering ubiquitous in ML notebooks.

timeline.label: Present — ML EDA baseline
timeline.description: Loss curves, histogram grids, and scatter diagnostics in Matplotlib precede specialized viz topics in modern ML curricula.
